title: HTTP Status Codes
description:
## Reading the Response

HTTP responses carry a numeric **status code** whose first digit indicates a class: **2xx** success, **3xx** redirect, **4xx** client error, **5xx** server error.

Your browser hits a site fronted by a reverse proxy and receives **`502 Bad Gateway`**. What does this most likely mean?

options.text: The client sent a malformed request and should fix and retry it.
The proxy/gateway received an invalid or no response from the **upstream** backend server it was trying to reach.
The page moved permanently and the client should follow a redirect.
The request succeeded and the resource is being returned normally.

explanation: `502 Bad Gateway` is a **5xx server-side error**: a server acting as a **gateway or proxy** got an invalid (or no) response from the **upstream** server it forwarded the request to — commonly the backend is down, crashed, or unreachable. It is *not* a client mistake (that would be a **4xx**, e.g. `400 Bad Request` or `404 Not Found`), not a redirect (**3xx**, e.g. `301 Moved Permanently`), and not success (**2xx**, e.g. `200 OK`). Note also **HTTP vs HTTPS**: HTTPS is HTTP carried over a TLS-encrypted connection — the status codes are identical, only the transport is secured.

hints.title: What does the leading digit tell you?
hints.body: 5xx means the server side failed. A gateway/proxy returning 5xx is reporting a problem talking to the server behind it, not a problem with your request.
